title: Case on Restrictions on Use of Personal Mobility Devices
text:
1Case on Restrictions on Use of Personal Mobility Devices
[2021Hun-Ma946, December 18, 2025]
* First Draft
In this case, the Court held that Article 43 and Article 50, Section (4) of the Road Traffic Act, 
inter alia, do not infringe general freedom of action or the right to equality. These provisions 
require a person intending to operate a personal mobility device to obtain a driver’s license 
and require the operator and any passenger to wear personal protective equipment. They also 
impose a criminal fine or other penalties for violations of such obligations.
Background of the Case
The National Assembly, through an amendment to the Road Traffic Act on January 12, 
2021 (Act No. 17891), restricted the operation of “personal mobility devices,” such as 
electric kick scooters, to persons who hold at least a license for a motorized bicycle, and at 
the same time established provisions allowing the imposition of administrative fines or other 
penalties for unlicensed operation or failure to wear personal protective equipment.
Complainants are individuals who ordinarily used electric kick scooters. Alleging that the 
above provisions prevented them from freely using electric kick scooters as of August 10, 
2021 and thereby infringed their general freedom of action and right to equality, 
Complainants filed the instant constitutional complaint.
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether the following provisions infringe 
Complainants’ fundamental rights: 
1) the portion of the phrase “motor vehicle, etc.” in both Article 43 and the main clause 
of Article 80, Section (1) of the Road Traffic Act (amended by Act No. 17891 on January 
12, 2021) concerning personal mobility devices, and Article 156, Item 13 of the former 
Road Traffic Act (amended by Act No. 17891 on January 12, 2021 and before amendment 
by Act No. 18491 on October 19, 2021) (hereinafter collectively referred to as the “License 
Provisions”); and 
2) the portion of the phrase “bicycle, etc.” in Article 50, Section (4) of the Road Traffic 
2Act (amended by Act No. 17891 on January 12, 2021) concerning personal mobility 
devices, and Article 156, Item 6 and Article 160, Section (2), Item 3 of the same Act 
insofar as they pertain to the portion of the phrase “bicycle, etc.” in Article 50, Section (4) 
concerning personal mobility devices (hereinafter collectively referred to as the “Protective 
Equipment Provisions”; together with the “License Provisions,” collectively referred to as 
the “Provisions at Issue”). The Provisions at Issue read as follows.
Provisions at Issue
Road Traffic Act (amended by Act No. 17891 on January 12. 2021)
Article 43 (Prohibition of unlicensed operation, etc.)
No person shall operate a motor vehicle, etc. without having obtained a driver’s license 
from the commissioner of a city or provincial police agency pursuant to Article 80, or while a 
driver’s license is suspended.
Article 50 (Matters to be observed by specific operators)
(4) When operating a bicycle, etc. on a bike lane or a road under the Road Act, the operator 
shall wear personal protective equipment prescribed by Decree of the Ministry of the Interior 
and Safety and require any passenger to wear such equipment.
Article 80 (Driver’s licenses)
(1) A person who intends to drive a motor vehicle, etc. shall obtain a driver’s license from 
the commissioner of a city or provincial police agency: Provided, That this shall not apply 
where a transportation disadvantaged person, as defined in Article 2, Section (1) of the Act 
on Promotion of the Mobility Convenience of Transportation Disadvantaged Persons, drives 
a motor-equipped vehicle defined in Article 2, Item 19, Sub-item (b), whose maximum speed 
does not exceed 20 kilometers per hour
Article 156 (Penalty provisions)
Any of the following persons shall be punished by a fine not exceeding 200,000 won, by 
misdemeanor imprisonment, or by a minor fine:
36. Any operator who, in violation of Article 50, Section (1), (3), or (4), has failed to 
fasten a seat belt or wear personal protective equipment (excluding bicycle operators).
 The former Road Traffic Act (amended by Act No. 17891 on January 12, 2021 and before 
amendment by Act No. 18491 on October 19, 2021)
Article 156 (Penalty provisions)
Any of the following persons shall be punished by a fine not exceeding 200,000 won, by 
misdemeanor imprisonment, or by a minor fine:
13. A person who, in violation of Article 43, has operated a personal mobility device 
without holding a driver’s license authorizing the operation of a motorized bicycle under 
Article 80 (including cases where such license has been suspended), or without having been 
issued an international driver’s license authorizing the operation of a motorized bicycle 
(including cases where operation is prohibited or the license has expired).
 
Road Traffic Act (amended by Act No. 17891 on January 12. 2021)
Article 160 (Administrative fines)
(2) Any of the following persons shall be subject to an administrative fine not exceeding 
200,000 won:
3. Any operator who, in violation of Article 50, Section (3) or (4), has failed to require 
any passenger to wear personal protective equipment (excluding bicycle operators).
Summary of the Decision
1. Discussion of justiciability
Some Complainants had obtained a Class 1 or Class 2 regular driver’s license before the 
License Provisions entered into force, and no special circumstances, such as the revocation of 
those licenses, are found. Therefore, as persons qualified under the Road Traffic Act to 
operate personal mobility devices, they are not subject to any restriction on their fundamental 
rights imposed by the License Provisions and thus fail to satisfy the requirement of 
4self-relatedness in alleging an infringement of fundamental rights. Accordingly, the claims of 
those Complainants with respect to the License Provisions are all dismissed as nonjusticiable.
2. Whether the Provisions at Issue infringe general freedom of action
The Provisions at Issue aim to prevent harm to the life and body of citizens, including 
personal mobility device operators, to ensure road traffic safety, and at the same time to foster 
and establish a culture of safe use of personal mobility devices.
Personal mobility devices lack separate safety equipment capable of adequately protecting 
operators, and their lightweight bodies and relatively small size significantly increase both the 
likelihood of accidents and the degree of injury to users in the event of an accident. Operators 
of personal mobility devices must possess a sufficient understanding of road traffic law and 
traffic rules. In addition, taking into account, inter alia, that knowledge of the structure and 
operating principles of the machines is also required, the License Provisions cannot be 
regarded as an excessive restriction.
In light of the high risk of personal mobility device accidents and the importance of 
protecting life and body, requiring the wearing of personal protective equipment through 
sanctioning measures such as administrative fines cannot be regarded as exceeding the 
bounds of legislative discretion.
Operators of personal mobility devices are subject to certain restrictions, such as the 
requirement to obtain a driver’s license and to wear personal protective equipment while 
operating the device. However, the disadvantages resulting from such restrictions cannot be 
regarded as excessive in comparison with the necessity and importance of the public interest.
3. Whether the Protective Equipment Provisions infringe the right to equality
Personal mobility devices share certain similarities with bicycles under the Act on 
Encouragement of Bicycle Riding, particularly electric bicycles, in terms of maximum speed 
and weight. However, unlike bicycles, personal mobility devices are essentially different 
from bicycles in that they are propelled solely by the power of an electric motor, and their 
structural characteristics and operating principles likewise differ from those of bicycles. In 
addition, considering both the various types of accident risks associated with the operation of 
personal mobility devices and the severity of the resulting harm, the need for operators and 
5passengers to wear personal protective equipment is particularly great.
Taking into account the characteristics of personal mobility devices, the legislature sought, 
unlike in the case of bicycles, to more effectively secure compliance with the obligation for 
operators and passengers to wear protective equipment by imposing sanctions such as 
administrative fines. This cannot be regarded as constituting manifestly unreasonable 
discrimination against operators of personal mobility devices.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
